kernel: The core of your operating system — the part that talks directly to your computer's hardware and manages all running programs.
daemon: A background program that runs automatically and quietly, handling tasks like managing your network or printing documents.
systemd: The system manager for most modern Linux computers — it starts background services when your computer boots up.
journalctl: A tool to read the system log — a record of everything that has happened on your computer.
journald: The part of systemd that collects and stores log messages from all background services.
systemctl: A tool to control background services — start, stop, restart, or check their status.
service: A background program that runs automatically and provides a specific function, like a web server or a database.
unit: In systemd, a file that describes a service, timer, or other resource the system manages.
apt: The package manager on Ubuntu and Debian — use it to install, update, and remove software.
dpkg: A low-level tool for managing software packages on Debian-based systems like Ubuntu.
snap: A way to install software in a self-contained bundle that works across many Linux versions.
flatpak: Another way to install applications that work across different Linux systems.
dnf: The package manager on Fedora and Red Hat — use it to install, update, and remove software.
pacman: The package manager on Arch Linux.
ppa: Personal Package Archive — a third-party software source you can add to get software not in the official Ubuntu store.
repository: A collection of software packages that your package manager can install from.
partition: A section of your hard drive or SSD treated as a separate storage area.
filesystem: The way your computer organises files on a drive — like a filing system for your storage.
mount: Connecting a drive or partition so your computer can use it.
unmount: Safely disconnecting a drive before removing it.
fstab: A file that tells your computer which drives to connect automatically when it starts up.
swap: Extra space on your drive used as overflow when your computer runs out of regular memory.
inode: An internal record your filesystem keeps for each file, storing its properties but not its name.
symlink: A shortcut that points to another file or folder.
permission: Rules that control who can read, write, or run a file.
chmod: A command to change who can read, write, or run a file.
chown: A command to change who owns a file or folder.
root: The most powerful user account on a Linux system — has permission to do anything.
sudo: A way to run a single command with administrator (root) permissions.
ssh: A secure way to connect to another computer over a network.
scp: A way to securely copy files between computers over a network.
rsync: A tool to efficiently copy or synchronise files, only transferring what has changed.
cron: A built-in scheduler that runs commands automatically at set times.
crontab: The file where you write your scheduled tasks for cron.
bash: The default command-line shell on most Linux systems — where you type commands.
shell: The program that accepts your commands and runs them — like bash or zsh.
terminal: The window where you type commands to control your computer.
process: A running program. Every app you open is one or more processes.
pid: Process ID — the unique number assigned to each running program.
kill: A command to stop a running program.
signal: A message sent to a running program to tell it to stop, pause, or reload.
stdout: The normal output of a command — what gets printed to your screen.
stderr: The error output of a command — error messages go here separately.
pipe: A way to feed the output of one command directly into another.
redirect: Sending the output of a command to a file instead of the screen.
environment variable: A named value your system and programs use for configuration, like your username or home folder.
path: A list of folders where your computer looks for programs when you type a command.
config file: A file that stores settings for a program.
log file: A file where a program records what it has been doing.
init: The very first process started when your computer boots — it starts everything else.
boot: The process of starting your computer from off.
grub: The boot loader — the menu you might see when starting up that lets you choose which system to boot.
kernel panic: A serious error where your computer's core software crashes and cannot recover — like a Blue Screen on Windows.
oom killer: A part of the kernel that kills programs to free memory when your computer runs out of RAM.
ram: Your computer's fast, temporary memory — used by running programs.
cpu: The processor — the main chip that does calculations in your computer.
gpu: The graphics chip — handles images, video, and increasingly, AI tasks.
disk: Your computer's permanent storage — where files are saved even when the computer is off.
ssd: A fast type of storage that uses chips instead of spinning disks.
hdd: A traditional hard drive that stores data on spinning magnetic disks — slower than an SSD.
nvme: A very fast type of SSD that connects directly to the motherboard.
network interface: A hardware or virtual connection to a network — like your Wi-Fi card or ethernet port.
ip address: A number that identifies your computer on a network, like an address.
dns: The system that translates website names (like google.com) into the numbers computers use to connect.
port: A number that identifies a specific service on a computer — for example, web servers use port 80.
firewall: Software that controls which network traffic is allowed in and out of your computer.
ufw: Uncomplicated Firewall — an easy-to-use firewall tool on Ubuntu.
iptables: A powerful but complex tool for controlling network traffic rules.
nftables: The modern replacement for iptables on newer Linux systems.
package: A bundle containing a program and everything it needs to run.
dependency: A program or library that another program needs in order to work.
library: Shared code that multiple programs use — installed once and reused.
executable: A file that can be run as a program.
script: A text file containing a series of commands that run in order.
alias: A shortcut you define for a longer command.
socket: A communication endpoint — programs use sockets to talk to each other or to the network.
tmpfs: A temporary filesystem stored in memory — its contents disappear when the computer restarts.
proc: A virtual filesystem at /proc that exposes information about running processes.
sys: A virtual filesystem at /sys that exposes information about hardware.
udev: The system that detects hardware devices and sets them up automatically.
module: A piece of code that can be loaded into the kernel to add support for hardware or features.
lsmod: A command that shows which kernel modules are currently loaded.
modprobe: A command to load or remove kernel modules.
dmesg: A log of kernel messages — useful for diagnosing hardware problems.
uptime: How long your computer has been running without a restart.
load average: A measure of how busy your computer's processor is — shown as three numbers for the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes.
top: A real-time view of running programs and how much CPU and memory they use.
htop: A friendlier, colour-coded version of top.
ps: A command that lists running programs.
df: A command that shows how much space is used and free on each drive.
du: A command that shows how much space a folder or file is using.
find: A command to search for files by name, size, date, or other properties.
grep: A command to search for text inside files.
sed: A command to find and replace text in files or command output.
awk: A command for processing and extracting text from structured data.
tar: A tool to bundle multiple files into one archive, often used with compression.
gzip: A common compression format for Linux files.
zip: A compression format that works on most operating systems.
wget: A tool to download files from the internet.
curl: A tool to transfer data from or to a server — often used to download files or test APIs.
nginx: A popular web server and reverse proxy.
apache: Another popular web server, often called Apache2 on Ubuntu.
mysql: A widely used open-source database server.
sqlite: A lightweight database stored in a single file — no server needed.
redis: A fast in-memory data store, often used as a cache.
docker: A tool to run programs in isolated containers — like lightweight virtual machines.
container: An isolated environment for running a program, sharing the computer's kernel but with its own files and settings.
virtual machine: A completely separate computer running inside your computer using software.
hypervisor: Software that creates and manages virtual machines.
kvm: A built-in Linux feature that lets you run virtual machines.
nvidia: A company that makes graphics cards — their Linux driver requires specific installation steps.
driver: Software that lets your operating system communicate with a piece of hardware.
xorg: The traditional display server for Linux — manages your screen and input devices.
wayland: A modern replacement for Xorg — more secure and efficient.
desktop environment: A graphical interface like GNOME or KDE that makes Linux easy to use with a mouse.
gnome: A popular desktop environment used by Ubuntu by default.
kde: Another popular desktop environment known for its customisation options.
xfce: A lightweight desktop environment that uses less memory.
display manager: The login screen — programs like GDM or LightDM handle this.
gdm: GNOME Display Manager — the login screen used by Ubuntu.
lightdm: A lightweight login screen used by several Linux distributions.
window manager: The part of your desktop that draws and controls windows.
tty: A text-only terminal session — you can switch to one with Ctrl+Alt+F2.
virtual console: A separate text-only session running alongside your graphical desktop.
locale: Language and regional settings that control how dates, numbers, and text are displayed.
ntp: Network Time Protocol — keeps your computer's clock accurate by syncing with time servers.
systemd-resolved: A background service that handles DNS lookups (translating website names to addresses).
networkmanager: A background service that manages your network connections.
netplan: A network configuration tool used by Ubuntu.
bluetooth: Wireless technology for short-range device connections.
pulseaudio: A sound server that manages audio on many Linux systems.
pipewire: A modern audio and video server replacing PulseAudio on newer systems.
alsa: The low-level audio system in the Linux kernel.
apparmor: A security system that restricts what programs can access.
selinux: A powerful security system used mainly on Red Hat-based systems.
seccomp: A kernel feature that limits which system calls a program can make — used for sandboxing.
cgroup: A kernel feature used to limit and track the resources a group of programs can use.
namespace: A kernel feature that gives programs an isolated view of the system.
ulimit: A limit on system resources a user or process can use.
oom: Out of Memory — what happens when your computer runs out of RAM.
swappiness: A setting that controls how eagerly your computer uses swap space.
hugepages: Large memory blocks that can improve performance for certain workloads.
transparent hugepages: An automatic version of hugepages that the kernel manages for you.
irq: Interrupt Request — a signal from hardware telling the CPU to pay attention to it.
cpu governor: A setting that controls how your CPU adjusts its speed for power saving or performance.
thermal throttling: When your CPU automatically slows down to prevent overheating.
watchdog: A timer that automatically restarts the system if it becomes unresponsive.
core dump: A file saved when a program crashes, containing a snapshot of its memory for debugging.
strace: A tool that shows every system call a running program makes — useful for diagnosing problems.
ltrace: Like strace but for library function calls.
valgrind: A tool for finding memory leaks and errors in programs.
gdb: The GNU Debugger — used to inspect and debug programs.
objdump: A tool for examining binary executable files.
ldd: Shows which shared libraries a program depends on.
ldconfig: Updates the system's library cache so programs can find shared libraries.
pkg-config: A tool that helps programs find the libraries they need during compilation.
make: A build tool that automates compiling software from source code.
gcc: The GNU C Compiler — used to build programs from C source code.
clang: An alternative C/C++ compiler known for clear error messages.
python: A popular programming language — also used by many Linux tools.
virtualenv: A tool to create isolated Python environments.
git: A version control system for tracking changes to files.
vim: A powerful text editor operated entirely from the keyboard.
nano: A simple, beginner-friendly text editor for the terminal.
emacs: A highly extensible text editor and environment.
less: A tool for reading long files or command output one screen at a time.
more: An older, simpler version of less.
man: The manual — type 'man command' to read documentation for any command.
tldr: Simplified, example-focused manual pages for common commands.
which: A command that shows where a program is installed.
whereis: Shows where a program's binary, source, and manual page are located.
type: Shows how a command is interpreted — whether it is a binary, alias, or shell built-in.
history: A list of commands you have previously typed in the terminal.
alias: A shortcut you define for a longer command.
export: Makes an environment variable available to child processes.
source: Runs a script in the current shell session rather than a new one.
xargs: Passes output from one command as arguments to another.
tee: Sends output to both the screen and a file at the same time.
nohup: Runs a command so it keeps running even after you close the terminal.
screen: A tool that keeps terminal sessions running when you disconnect.
tmux: A modern terminal multiplexer — run multiple terminals in one window.
at: Schedules a command to run once at a specific time.
batch: Schedules a command to run when the system load is low.
lsof: Lists open files — shows which programs have which files open.
ss: Shows active network connections — the modern replacement for netstat.
netstat: An older tool for showing network connections.
ip: A powerful tool for managing network interfaces and routing.
ifconfig: An older tool for viewing and configuring network interfaces.
ping: Sends a test signal to a computer to check if it is reachable.
traceroute: Shows the path network traffic takes to reach a destination.
dig: A tool for querying DNS servers.
arp: Shows the mapping between IP addresses and hardware addresses on your local network.
route: Shows or modifies the network routing table.
tc: Traffic control — used to manage network bandwidth.
fail2ban: A tool that automatically blocks IP addresses that make too many failed login attempts.
uname: Shows information about the operating system and kernel.
hostname: Your computer's name on the network.
hostnamectl: A tool to view and change your computer's hostname.
timedatectl: A tool to view and set the system time and timezone.
localectl: A tool to view and set the system locale and keyboard layout.
lspci: Lists PCI devices — graphics cards, network cards, etc.
lsusb: Lists USB devices connected to your computer.
lshw: Lists all hardware in your computer.
dmidecode: Reads hardware information from the system firmware.
sensors: Shows temperature and fan speed readings from hardware sensors.
smartctl: Checks the health of hard drives and SSDs.
hdparm: A tool for checking and tuning hard drive performance.
badblocks: Scans a drive for bad sectors.
fsck: Checks and repairs a filesystem — like running chkdsk on Windows.
mkfs: Creates a new filesystem on a drive or partition.
fdisk: A tool for viewing and editing disk partition tables.
parted: A more capable disk partitioning tool.
lvm: Logical Volume Manager — lets you resize and manage disk partitions flexibly.
raid: Redundant Array of Independent Disks — combines multiple drives for speed or data protection.
btrfs: A modern Linux filesystem with built-in snapshots and error checking.
zfs: A powerful filesystem known for data integrity and snapshots.
xfs: A high-performance filesystem used by Red Hat and others.
ntfs: The Windows filesystem — Linux can read and write it.
exfat: A filesystem often used for large USB drives and SD cards.
fat32: An older, universally compatible filesystem for small USB drives.
cryptsetup: A tool to create and manage encrypted drives.
luks: Linux Unified Key Setup — the standard way to encrypt drives on Linux.
gpg: GNU Privacy Guard — encrypts files and verifies their authenticity.
openssl: A toolkit for encryption and creating certificates.
ssl: Secure Sockets Layer — the technology behind HTTPS encryption.
tls: Transport Layer Security — the modern version of SSL.
certificate: A digital document that proves a server or person is who they claim to be.
ca-certificates: A package containing trusted certificate authorities — used to verify secure connections.
